CSCE 312 Syllabus

Section 500 (37714)

Computer Organization

Spring 2026 - College Station

Instructor Details

Aakash Tyagi

Email:  tyagi@tamu.edu

Office Location:  PETR-207

Phone:  979-845-5480

Office Hours

Book time with Tyagi, Aakash: 1o1 with Aakash Tyagi

Preferred Contact Method

Use the above link (Wed, Fri)

Webpage:  https://engineering.tamu.edu/cse/profiles/tyagi-aakash.html

Biography

Aakash Tyagi has a PhD in Computer Engineering in the field of Semiconductor Yield

Modeling and Design for Manufacturability. He has over two decades of industry

experience at Intel Corporation in end-to-end CPU development including front-end

logic design, functional verification, physical design, circuit design, and silicon debug

and validation. Since 2014, he has been with Texas A&M University Department of

Computer Science and Engineering as a Professor of Practice where he teaches

courses in Computer Organization, Systems Programming, and Software Testing

and leads research and workforce development activities in hardware verification.

Catalog Description

Computer Organization. (3-2). Credit 4. Computer systems from programmer's

perspective including simple logic design, data representation and processor

architecture, programming of processors, memory, control flow, input/output, and

performance measurements; hands-on lab assignments. Prerequisite: Grade of C

or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

Additional Course Details

You are required to have a computing device on which you have permission to

install software. Please contact the instructor if you do not have a computing device.

Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) is an initiative in the college of engineering where

students are required to bring their own computing device to class. The following

link explains the program and provides information about approved

devices: https://engineering.tamu.edu/easa/areas/academics/byod . Approved BYOD

devices fulfill the requirements for this class, although many other devices are

Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) is an initiative in the college of engineering where

students are required to bring their own computing device to class. The following

link explains the program and provides information about approved

devices: https://engineering.tamu.edu/easa/areas/academics/byod . Approved BYOD

devices fulfill the requirements for this class, although many other devices are

sufficient.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent

enrollment.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Perform basic logic design

Understand the design of a simple computer system

Understand elements that make up a CPU (Central Processing Unit)

Design software with an understanding of how it will run on computers

Participate in discussions that involve impact of hardware and software

elements on each other and the performance of the program

Take intermediate and advanced classes in computer architecture, systems,

operating systems, cloud, parallel computing

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Recommended

The Elements of Computing Systems, Second Edition, Building a Modern Computer

from First Principles

Authors:  Noam Nisan, Shimon Schocken

Publisher:  MIT Press

Publication Date:  2021

Edition:  2nd edition

URL for Resource:  https://www.nand2tetris.org/

Notes:

We cover the first half of this book. The chapters are available free online at the

above link.

This material Is: Optional

Digital Design

Authors:  Frank Vahid

Publisher:  Wiley

Publication Date:  2010

Edition:  2nd Edition

Notes:

We use content from this book for teaching logic design.

This material Is: Optional

Computer Systems: A Programmer's Perspective

Authors:  Randal E. Bryant and David R. O'Hallaron

Publisher:  Prentice Hall

Publication Date:  2015

Notes:

We refer to this book for content on memory hierarchy

Grading Policy

Grade Allocation (% of total)

Graded Lab Exercises (12): 48%

Lab Attendance: 6%

Project (1): 16%

Midterm Exam (1): 15%

Final Exam (1): 15%

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Exams : Absence is marked as 0 without prior approval from the instructor. Absence

requests must be made in email (tyagi@tamu.edu) at least 4 hour in advance.

Absence requests without accompanying physician's note may be denied.

Project : Late Submissions will incur a flat 30% penalty. Late submissions will be

allowed till 11:59pm on the last day of classes.

Graded Lab Exercises (GLE) :  Late Submissions will incur a flat 10% penalty. Late

submissions will be allowed till 11:59pm on the Sunday of the week during which the

Absence requests without accompanying physician's note may be denied.

Project : Late Submissions will incur a flat 30% penalty. Late submissions will be

allowed till 11:59pm on the last day of classes.

Graded Lab Exercises (GLE) :  Late Submissions will incur a flat 10% penalty. Late

submissions will be allowed till 11:59pm on the Sunday of the week during which the

GLE was assigned.

Course Schedule

TENTATIVE SCHEDULE (Subject to change during the

semester)

Lab Themes  (GLE* are graded lab exercises handed out on

WeekX.Day1 and due by 11:59pm WeekX.Day2, so as example if labs

are MW (TR) then GLE is assigned on Mon(Tue) and is due for

submission by 11:59pm Wed(Thu) of Week X.

WEEK1: No LAB

WEEK2: Tool Installation + GLE1 (TBD)

WEEK3: Writing basic hdl (Inverter using NAND gate) + Writing test file

+ Writing cmp file + GLE2 (Basic Gates)

WEEK3: Building multi-inputs, multi-bit gates + GLE3 (multi-input, multi-

bit gates, simple logic expression implementations using

combinational logic)

WEEK4: MUX and Decoder + GLE4 (Modular combinational selector

logic)

WEEK5: Debugging failures + GLE5 (Meaty Combinational logic

implementation)

WEEK6: Basic Addition Building Blocks + GLE6 (Adders)

WEEK7: Ripple Carry Addition, Sub-busing + GLE7 (Arithmetic Logic)

===============================MIDTERM================

============

WEEK8: Clocking and BIT + GLE8 (Multi-bit Registers and Modular

Memory)

WEEK9: Modular memory + GLE9 (Sequential Logic)

WEEK10: Basic assembly code + GLE10 (Basic Assembly)

WEEK11: Code with variables + GLE11 (Assembly code with variables)

WEEK12: Code with conditionals and labels + GLE12 (Assembly code

with conditionals and labels)

WEEK13: BITBOT CPU (Deploy Project)

WEEK14: BITBOT Computer (Continue Project)

Lecture Themes

WEEK1: Course Intro and Big Picture of Modern Computers, Basic

Number Systems

WEEK2: Boolean Algebra, Properties and Minimization, Logic Gates

and Expressions

WEEK3: Combinational Logic Design, Combinational Logic Delays

WEEK4: MUX, DMUX, Decoder, Encoder

WEEK5: Basic Addition Building Blocks + Ripple Carry Adder

WEEK6: Modular Adders

WEEK7: 2s complement and Subtractors

===============================MIDTERM================

=============

WEEK8: Sequential Design, DFF, Bit, Registers

WEEK9: Modular memory

WEEK9: Assembly Language, Variables, Labels

WEEK10: Computer Architecture

WEEK11: Computer Architecture

WEEK12: Special Topics: Memory Hierarchy

WEEK13: Special Topics: Pipelining

WEEK14: Special Topics: Parallel Processing

AI Statement

You are permitted to use AI as your assistant and tutor where applicable and

with caution. Do not outsource your graded work to AI.
